message:
docs(gists): deep introductions for ADR-005/006/155/156

Four extended gists in the same 9-section voice as the rvDNA / ruQu
deep gists, each citing source files at file:line.

- ipfs-backend-deep.md (ADR-005): bundle-only IPFS backend, three
  modes (kubo / gateway-only / kubo+fallback), CID-witness binding
  enforced via the R-IPFS-1 hard refuse, five operator-visible audit
  codes mirrored in the Console.
- console-deep.md (ADR-006): tri-mode Vite/React/TS SPA (Demo /
  WASM-local / Live MCP), 7 routes, 15 iterations on
  research/management-ui, smoke harness as the regression baseline.
- datalake-layer-deep.md (ADR-155): cache-first reframe, BackendAdapter
  trait + RaBitQ-native cache + bundle sidecar, M1 numbers from
  BENCHMARK.md, M2/M3 substrate adapters in shipping order.
- memory-substrate-deep.md (ADR-156): positioning ratification — the
  six-guarantee acceptance test, memory_class tag (deliberately not
  in the witness), what NOT to absorb (compact stays in RVM/Cognitum).

@@ -0,0 +1,112 @@
+# ruLake Console — A Deep Introduction
+
+## TL;DR
+
+The ruLake Console is a Vite + React + TypeScript SPA at `ui/`, deployed to GitHub Pages, that turns the project from "clone the repo, install Rust, run a server" into "open a URL, click Send, see a SHAKE-256 witness recompute in your tab." It is tri-mode by construction — Demo (bundled fixtures), WASM-local (the user's real bundles persisted in IndexedDB and cryptographically verified by `rulake-wasm` in-browser, no server required), and Live (Streamable-HTTP MCP against a remote `rulake-mcp` instance) — with all three modes sharing every screen and only the data source differing. Fifteen iterations on branch `research/management-ui` have produced a build that smoke-tests green from zero (`ui/scripts/smoke.sh`), runs against an in-process mcp-server end-to-end, and exercises 7 audit codes plus 5 refusal codes through real wasm calls and real wire calls — no `setTimeout` placeholders.
+
+## Introduction
+
+ruLake had reached a point where the bottleneck was no longer the runtime. The crate was on crates.io at v2.2.0; the npm umbrella was rolling out; `mcp-server v0.8` was shipping Streamable HTTP, JSON-RPC, four authentication modes, JWT-scope-driven RBAC, JWKS hot rotation, IPFS-aware bundle resources, and an in-process JSONL audit log; `rulake-wasm` exposed `verifyBundleJson`, `computeWitness`, `searchBruteForceL2` at ~149 KB compressed; the `gcs-backend` and `ipfs-backend` siblings were live. What was missing was a UI — the closest thing the project had was a one-page witness-verifier proof of concept at `examples/wasm/01-witness-verifier-browser/`. Every adoption path went through the README and a `cargo install`.
+
+The prior research note (`docs/research/management-ui.md`, 1,457 lines) had given a "conditional yes" to building a UI, gated on three things: an operator persona asking for a console in writing, an agent-developer persona bottlenecked on onboarding friction in measurable ways, and 3 engineer-weeks of capacity that wasn't better spent elsewhere. The new fact was that the operator-and-evangelist persona had shown up not with a brief but with code — a 5.0 MB design artefact (`assets/RuLake dashboard.zip`, 22 files) that was already a working in-browser console with six routes, an IndexedDB store, a multi-step welcome wizard, contextual help on every screen, three theme presets, an ambient vector substrate, the receipt-printer witness aesthetic, and production-grade SEO meta. The artefact was the resolution of the gating question. ADR-006's job was to convert it into a production deployment with the lowest-friction stack.
+
+The reason a browser console matters now, rather than later, is structural. ruLake's pitch — content-addressed, witness-anchored, federation-ready vector memory — is not something a screenshot can convey. A first-time visitor needs to *see* the witness recompute. They need to paste a CID and watch the bundle verify in their tab. They need to send a query and watch the receipt-printer wax stamp light up `● MATCH` because `verifyBundleJson` ran in their browser, not because a server told them so. That experience does not exist in any other vector-database product, and the GitHub Pages URL is the smallest surface that carries it.
+
+The harder, less obvious decision in ADR-006 is the third mode. Demo and Live are the obvious pair — fixture-rendered for the 30-second pitch, server-backed for production operators. The third mode, **WASM-local**, was an upgrade of the original decision (3) once the dependency closure clarified: IndexedDB + `rulake-wasm` + Web Workers + WebGL + an embedding-provider plug-in is enough substrate for a developer to use ruLake as their agent's actual memory layer with no infrastructure at all. LangChain.js, browser agents, Cloudflare Workers RAG, Bun servers — all consume bundles persisted in IndexedDB, indexed by witness, distributed by IPFS pin, with the cryptographic provenance the others cannot offer. WASM-local is the mode that turns the Console from a dashboard into a substrate.
+
+The three modes share every screen; only the data source differs. The witness-verification path is identical and load-bearing across all three (the load-bearing decision in ADR-006 §8): every server response carrying a witness is locally re-verified by `verifyBundleJson` from `rulake-wasm` *before* it is rendered as trusted, and a divergence flips the receipt stamp amber rather than rendering the response. The network is untrusted; the cryptography is the only trust boundary the user is asked to extend.
+
+## The decision in detail
+
+What got picked. **Vite 5** as the build tool — the operator's brief explicitly asked for it, and it is the right tool for a static SPA with lazy WASM. **React 18.3** for the JSX→TSX port from the design artefact. **TypeScript 5.4 strict** so every fixture and every server response gets a typed boundary. **`wouter` for routing** — 1.5 KB, the design is single-string route state, no need for react-router's machinery. **No state library** — local `useState` plus DOM custom events plus the existing `useRuStore` IndexedDB hook is enough for seven routes. **No component library** — the design is bespoke; CSS modules or per-component plain CSS imports keep it that way without fighting the receipt-printer aesthetic. **`zod` for response-schema validation** at the wire boundary. **`vite-plugin-wasm` plus dynamic `import('rulake-wasm')`** so the ~149 KB wasm chunk only loads on routes that need it (Playground, Bundle). **Production dependency closure: 6 packages** — react, react-dom, wouter, zod, rulake, rulake-wasm. Tree-shaken bundle target under 250 KB pre-wasm.
+
+What got rejected. Tailwind — would either pay for nothing (the design is bespoke) or fight the receipt-printer aesthetic. CSS-in-JS — same reason; vanilla CSS and per-component imports preserve the design's voice. Zustand / Jotai — overkill for the route count. A `gh-pages` branch — the official `actions/deploy-pages@v4` flow publishes via the `id-token` mechanism without one. mTLS in the browser — the TLS handshake picks the certificate before any JS runs, so the in-browser path cannot satisfy mTLS; the design correctly disables that option (`screens.jsx:1237`-`1245`) and the production console keeps that exact warning. Token persistence — full bearer / JWT tokens never persist; only the first 8 chars are stored in IndexedDB for display, and the full token lives in JS memory only and is re-pasted on tab reload (`screens.jsx:1152`).
+
+The load-bearing trade-offs. **Witness re-verification is unconditional.** Even in Live mode against a trusted operator's mcp-server, the response is parsed, the witness is recomputed locally with `verifyBundleJson`, and a mismatch refuses to render the response as trusted (the receipt stamp turns amber, an audit row is appended, a toast surfaces the mismatch). The cost is a SHAKE-256 over a 300-byte bundle — sub-millisecond — and the win is that the network is no longer in the trust path. **WASM-local owns the user's API keys.** The OpenAI / Cohere / Voyage embedding key sits in JS memory (never persisted, never sent anywhere except the embedding provider) so a hostile browser extension or a tampered console asset *could* exfiltrate. The mitigation is CSP `connect-src` allow-listing the embedding endpoints, the witness chain verifying the console build itself when published as an IPFS-pinned bundle, and the welcome modal documenting the trust boundary in plain language. **WebGL renderers ship an SVG fallback.** Every WebGL component (VectorFieldGL, HistogramGL) carries an SVG fallback for hosts without `WebGL2RenderingContext`. Two implementations of the same chart is the cost; the WebGL win at ≥10k-vector renders is real. **IPFS optional path defaults off.** A default-on kubo URL would 404 noisily for every visitor without a local daemon; the welcome modal points at a three-line "enable IPFS" walkthrough instead.
+
+## Capabilities
+
+**Seven routes, three modes.** Stats / Playground / Browse / Bundle / Audit / Connect / App Store, each rendering identically across Demo / WASM-local / Live and only the data source differing. The mode picker on the Connect screen flips the bootstrap path: Demo loads from `data.js` fixtures, WASM-local mounts the IndexedDB store and the wasm module, Live constructs a `RuLakeHttp` client against a pasted endpoint and a pasted bearer/JWT.
+
+**Real cryptography in Demo mode.** The 700 ms / 850 ms `setTimeout` placeholders from the design artefact were replaced in iteration 1 (commit `7046e1a`) with actual `rulake-wasm` calls. The Bundle screen's "Recompute witness" button runs `computeWitness` over the visible bundle; the Playground's "Send" button runs `searchBruteForceL2` over a fixture vector set. The witness `MATCH` badge in Demo mode is the same SHAKE-256 verdict the Live console shows — only the *data* is synthetic, not the *proof*.
+
+**WASM-local IPFS round-trip.** Iteration 7 (commit `21b0b3a`) wired the Bundle screen to fetch a CIDv1 over a public IPFS gateway, verify the bundle's witness, and refuse on mismatch. The five audit codes for the fetch state machine — `IPFS_OK`, `IPFS_HTTP_ERROR`, `IPFS_NETWORK_ERROR`, `IPFS_FETCH_FAILED`, `IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED`, `IPFS_WITNESS_MISMATCH` — mirror the `ipfs-backend/` audit codes so the browser's audit ring and the server's structured logs share a vocabulary. Iteration 8 (`cbd6431`) ships a sample bundle at `ui/public/sample-bundle.json` and a "Try sample" button so the success path lands offline without a working IPFS path.
+
+**Live MCP wire round-trip.** Iteration 12 (commit `26dbe2b`) shipped `mcp-server v0.9 rulake_list_collections`; iteration 13 (`2204350`) wired the Browse screen's `Refresh` button to dispatch the real wire call and emit `LIST_COLLECTIONS_OK`; iteration 14 (`95f2251`) added the live-mode topbar pill (`● LIVE · host` / `○ DEMO · no live MCP`) and swapped the Browse table from fixture to live data on success. The smoke harness (`--live` flag) boots a local mcp-server, exercises the wire end-to-end, and asserts both `INIT_OK` and `LIST_COLLECTIONS_OK` land in the audit ledger.
+
+**Worked example — the 30-second pitch.** A first-time visitor lands on `https://ruvnet.github.io/RuLake/`, dismisses the welcome modal, navigates to Playground, hits Send. The Playground encodes a `tools/call` payload visibly so the developer can copy it into their own client. `searchBruteForceL2` runs against a fixture vector set in the WASM module instantiated in a Web Worker (commit `4a33405`'s `searchOffload.js` + `search.worker.js`); the result histogram renders in WebGL (commit `1a49c01` — `data-renderer="webgl2"`); the response carries a witness; `verifyBundleJson` recomputes the SHAKE-256 in the worker; the receipt-printer wax stamp lights up green with `● MATCH`. Five clicks, no install, zero accounts, all open source, the cryptography is real. ADR-006 §9 makes this the verification target: three people who have not used ruLake before, sat in front of a stopwatch, must reach a `verifyBundleJson MATCH` within 60 seconds.
+
+## Trust & correctness contract
+
+The contract is the WASM-local-first / no-server-needed mode. Three commitments, each cited.
+
+**Cryptography is real in all three modes.** Demo, WASM-local, and Live all run the same `verifyBundleJson` from `rulake-wasm@2.2.1` over every witness-bearing response before rendering it as trusted. The `setTimeout` placeholders that the design artefact carried as crypto-stubs were excised in iteration 1 (commit `7046e1a`); the Playground's `Send` and the Bundle's `Recompute witness` paths bind directly to `node-wasm/src/lib.rs:157` (`verifyBundleJson`), `node-wasm/src/lib.rs:211` (`computeWitness`), and `node-wasm/src/lib.rs:275` (`searchBruteForceL2`). A divergence between the published witness and the recomputed one flips the receipt stamp amber, appends an audit row to the local IndexedDB ring, and surfaces a toast — the response is *not* rendered as trusted (`screens.jsx:1168`, `:1228`).
+
+**WASM-local is a complete substrate, not a demo.** The Connect screen's mode picker (`screens.jsx:1140`-`1301`) gates an entire substrate, not a fixture switch. WASM-local mounts the IndexedDB store (`ui/src/lib/store.js`, the `RuStore` over five named stores), instantiates `rulake-wasm` for `searchBruteForceL2`/`verifyBundleJson`/`computeWitness`, optionally mounts a Web Worker for off-main-thread search (`screens.jsx:1775`-area; `searchOffload.js` + `search.worker.js`), optionally mounts a WebGL renderer for ≥10k-vector charts (`VectorFieldGL.jsx`, `data-renderer="webgl2"` vs `"2d"`), optionally calls an embedding provider (`ui/src/lib/embed/`, OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage with `EMBED_FAILED` on bad keys), and optionally fetches bundles from IPFS through the gateway/kubo path (`ui/src/lib/storage/ipfs.ts`). Every toggle is observable at runtime: 6/6 Storage settings are exposed via `data-*` attributes that the smoke suite asserts on.
+
+**No server is required for any user-facing claim.** The GitHub Pages URL defaults to Demo; WASM-local activates with two clicks; Live requires a pasted endpoint. The browser-only surface for an agent developer is complete: paste embeddings, search them with `searchBruteForceL2`, persist them in IndexedDB, publish a bundle to IPFS, share the CIDv1 with a peer who verifies the same witness in their tab. No `cargo install`, no `pip`, no MCP server, no auth provider. The smoke harness (`ui/scripts/smoke.sh`, 303 lines) exercises this path end-to-end with no live MCP — every assertion that does not carry the `--live` flag runs against the WASM-local substrate alone.
+
+The contract is operationally enforced by the smoke suite. From a fresh checkout, `ui/scripts/smoke.sh` builds the SPA, boots the preview server, opens an agent-browser session, clears IndexedDB, asserts the React bootstrap reaches 1 root child, dispatches Bundle.recompute → Bundle.try-sample → Playground.send → Connect.ipfs-probe → Connect.test (port 65535, expected `CONNECT_FAILED`), and asserts the audit ring contains `WITNESS_MATCH`, `IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED`, `OK_VERIFIED`, `IPFS_OK`, `CONNECT_FAILED`. With `--live`, it additionally boots `mcp-server` on port 9099, asserts a CORS preflight returns 204, fires Connect.test → Browse.refresh, and asserts `INIT_OK` and `LIST_COLLECTIONS_OK` land. Zero browser console errors across all 15 iterations is the canonical regression baseline (`ui/scripts/smoke.sh:288`-`294`).
+
+## Reference implementation status
+
+What shipped through 15 iterations on branch `research/management-ui`, with commits.
+
+| # | Commit | Deliverable |
+|---|--------|-------------|
+| 0 | `00ed0ca` | Vite + React scaffold; 9 design files ported via dynamic-import bootstrap; 6 routes navigate; full SEO meta in index.html |
+| 1 | `7046e1a` | Real `rulake-wasm::computeWitness` + `searchBruteForceL2` wired into Bundle.recompute + Playground.send (replaces 4 setTimeout placeholders) |
+| 2 | `5b956a9` | Connect.test → real `RuLakeHttp.connect()` over Streamable HTTP; mcp-server v0.9 CORS layer added |
+| 3 | `474112f` | WASM-local Storage settings card (embed/cloud/IPFS/WebGL/Workers/cache toggles, all persisted to IDB) |
+| 4 | `4a33405` | Web Worker offload — `searchOffload.js` + `search.worker.js`; audit-code suffix `_WORKER` |
+| 5 | `cf4b5b4` | Live sidebar audit count + new mcp-server CORS regression test |
+| 6 | `780ad26` | Real OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage embedding provider call from Playground; `EMBED_FAILED` audit row on bad keys |
+| 7 | `21b0b3a` | IPFS bundle fetch on Bundle screen — paste CIDv1, fetch via gateway, recompute witness; 5 audit codes for the fetch state machine |
+| 8 | `cbd6431` | Sample bundle at `ui/public/sample-bundle.json`; "Try sample" button → `IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED` success path lands offline |
+| 9 | `1a49c01` | WebGL toggle observable on VectorField (`data-renderer="webgl2"` vs `"2d"`); 6/6 Storage toggles runtime-observable |
+| 10 | `19f8453` | `ui/scripts/smoke.sh` (303 lines) — canonical regression baseline; green from 0 in one command |
+| 11 | `b57f7c7` | Smoke `--live` flag + CI integration in release-ui workflow; `INIT_OK` audit row landing on real handshake |
+| 12 | `26dbe2b` | mcp-server v0.9 `rulake_list_collections` tool |
+| 13 | `2204350` | Browse.refresh → real `rulake_list_collections` over wire; `LIST_COLLECTIONS_OK` audit code |
+| 14 | `95f2251` | Topbar live-mode pill (`● LIVE · host` / `○ DEMO · no live MCP`); Browse table swaps fixture → live data on success |
+
+Numbers as of iteration 15. Browser console errors: 0 across all 15 iterations. Audit codes exercised end-to-end: 7 (`WITNESS_MATCH`, `IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED`, `OK_VERIFIED_{MAIN,WORKER}`, `IPFS_OK`, `CONNECT_FAILED`, `INIT_OK`, `LIST_COLLECTIONS_OK`) plus 5 refusal codes (`EMBED_FAILED`, `IPFS_HTTP_ERROR`, `IPFS_NETWORK_ERROR`, `IPFS_FETCH_FAILED`, `IPFS_WITNESS_MISMATCH`). Storage settings runtime-observable: 6/6. mcp-server tools shipped: 8 (3 Read tier, 2 Publish, 3 Admin). mcp-server tests: 65 + 1 ignored. Smoke assertions: 15 (build, preview, mcp-server, browser session, bootstrap × 10 globals, action chain × 7, audit ledger × 7, topbar data-mode, console errors).
+
+Server-side gap status against deep-review §V. Gap 1 (`rulake_list_collections`) shipped in commit `26dbe2b`. Gap 3 (CORS allow-list for GH Pages origin) shipped permissive; allow-list-tightening is v0.10. Gap 5 (Embedding story for Playground) shipped — user-supplied OpenAI/Cohere/Voyage key in JS memory only. Gap 2 (`rulake://audit/tail`) deferred to mcp-server v0.10 (since landed at commit `e2c2402`). Gap 4 (`rulake://bundle/{b}/{c}` byte-stable JSON audit) deferred — no client uses it yet.
+
+What stayed out of v0.1 per Decision 9: PKCE for JWT, snapshot upload over HTTP, RBAC mutation, federation builder, full WebGL point-sprite renderer, cloud-bundle-storage runtime hookup (persisted-only — needs the user's signed bucket URL).
+
+## Composition with the rest of ruLake
+
+The Console is the user-facing apex of the stack and a strict consumer of every other layer.
+
+It consumes `rulake-wasm` for cryptography. The wasm module exports four primitives — `verifyBundleJson` (`node-wasm/src/lib.rs:157`), `computeWitness` (`node-wasm/src/lib.rs:211`), `searchBruteForceL2` (`node-wasm/src/lib.rs:275`), `formatVersion`/`buildInfo` (`node-wasm/src/lib.rs:343`) — that the Console binds to the witness-bearing surfaces (Bundle, Playground) under lazy import. The wasm chunk only loads on routes that touch crypto; Stats/Audit/Connect never trigger it.
+
+It consumes `mcp-server` for the wire. The Live mode constructs a `RuLakeHttp` client (`node/http.mjs:107`-`184`) against a pasted endpoint, and dispatches MCP tool calls — `rulake_query` for Playground, `rulake_list_collections` for Browse, `rulake://stats` for Stats, `rulake://audit/tail` for Audit (v0.10), `rulake://bundle/...` for Bundle. The capability-tier matrix (`mcp-server/src/server.rs:455`-`469`) and the `tools/list` visibility filter (`mcp-server/src/server.rs:566`-`585`) drive the Connect screen's capability pill.
+
+It consumes `ipfs-backend/` semantically. The browser's IPFS path (`ui/src/lib/storage/ipfs.ts`) mirrors the three modes (kubo / gateway-only / kubo + gateway-fallback) and the audit-code vocabulary (`IPFS_OK`, `IPFS_HTTP_ERROR`, `IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED`, `IPFS_FETCH_FAILED`, `IPFS_WITNESS_MISMATCH` — the last two tracked separately from the server-side `IPFS_BUNDLE_CID_MISMATCH` because the browser cannot enforce the CID↔witness binding without parsing the bundle, which is what `verifyBundleJson` does).
+
+It composes with `rvdna-backend/` and `ruqu-backend/` through the App Store screen (`ui/src/components/screens.jsx:2018`-`2141`). Each substrate gets a card listing install commands (Rust / MCP / npm / wasm), the ADR that governs it, status (Shipping / Scaffolded / Roadmap), the MCP tools it adds, and the research path. The seventh sidebar entry ("App store" — `screens.jsx:32`) lists the four substrates currently registered: rvDNA v2 (scaffolded, ADR-007), ruQu v2 (scaffolded, ADR-008), gcs-backend (shipping, ADR-155), ipfs-backend (shipping, ADR-005).
+
+A consumer, in this framing, is a thing that binds wasm primitives and wire calls into a UI without relaxing the witness contract. The Console's job is not to invent UX patterns or to compete with vector-DB dashboards on feature surface; it is to make the cryptographic claim visible in five clicks.
+
+## Open questions
+
+PKCE for JWT in Live mode (deferred to v0.2). A hosted public-demo `rulake-mcp` endpoint (deliberately not shipped — the GH Pages URL defaults to Demo, never Live, until the user pastes their own endpoint). Cloud-bundle-storage runtime hookup (toggle persisted; needs the user's signed bucket URL to cross from setting to flow). Peer-to-peer cache sharing beyond IPFS-published bundles (would need WebRTC + a witness discovery protocol — a v0.3 idea). A federation builder UI (deferred — single-process WASM-local mode is single-shard by definition; the federation primitive lives server-side). Lighthouse Performance ≥ 90 on the GH Pages URL on the mobile profile (asserted in CI but not yet measured against the production deploy). The trade-off where a hostile browser extension or a tampered console asset could exfiltrate an API key from JS memory — mitigated by CSP and the witness chain on the published console build, but the user owns the trade-off.
+

@@ -0,0 +1,105 @@
+# ruLake Datalake Layer — A Deep Introduction
+
+## TL;DR
+
+ADR-155 ratifies ruLake as a **vector execution cache with deterministic compression and federated refill** — a single-sentence positioning that says the cache hit path is the entire product surface, RaBitQ 1-bit codes plus a SHAKE-256 witness make cross-process cache sharing safe without comparing payloads, and federation is the *refill mechanism* on miss rather than the product shape. M1 shipped the `BackendAdapter` trait, `LocalBackend`, `FsBackend`, the witness-addressed cache with cross-backend dedup, rayon-parallel fan-out with adaptive per-shard rerank, and the bundle sidecar protocol; M2 added `gcs-backend` and `ipfs-backend`; M3 added the `rvdna-backend` and `ruqu-backend` substrates plus their MCP companions. The crate is published to crates.io as `rulake@2.2.0` with a measured 1.02× tax on `LocalBackend` cache hits versus direct `RabitqPlusIndex::search`.
+
+## Introduction
+
+The RVF ecosystem at `vendor/ruvector/` had been shipping the right primitives for years: append-only segment model with a tail manifest (RVF Four Laws), progressive HNSW indexing (Layer A/B/C), temperature-tiered quantization, RaBitQ 1-bit rotation-based quantization (`crates/ruvector-rabitq`, 100% recall@10 at 957 QPS single-thread on n=100k D=128 rerank×20), witness chains (SHAKE-256, Ed25519, ML-DSA-65 PQ), federation primitives (PII stripping, differential privacy, RDP accounting, FedAvg), an HTTP/SSE streaming server. What was missing was the layer that turned those primitives into something an enterprise customer could *adopt* — a thing they could put in front of the data they already had, in the warehouse they already used, with the governance they already enforced.
+
+The first cut of this ADR proposed one deep per-lake integration (BigQuery Tier-1 via external tables and remote functions) and treated the others as Tier-2 follow-ups. That shape works but multiplies the per-backend engineering linearly: each new backend gets its own governance, lineage, and auth story; the RaBitQ compression story lives N times instead of once; users with vectors *spread across* BigQuery and S3 still cannot federate. The reframe — and the reason ADR-155 v2 exists — was that the measured intermediary tax on `LocalBackend` is 1.02× direct RaBitQ. The abstraction is not a bottleneck. We can afford to put orchestration, governance, and routing on top, and the per-lake integration becomes a *push-down optimisation inside the adapter*, not a new product shape.
+
+The v3 reframe (2026-04-22) sharpened this further. The five-axis decision matrix in `docs/research/ruLake/06-positioning.md` (Latency, Simplicity, Governance, Adoption friction, Differentiation) gives cache-first 4 of 5 axes; federation-first wins only on Governance, and even there cache-first scores 4/5 because the cache is the choke point governance lives at. So ruLake stopped being "a federation engine that happens to cache" and became "a cache that happens to federate on miss" — a small change in framing that produces a much smaller crate, a much narrower contract, and a much sharper acceptance test (M1.5: ≥95% of queries return exact top-k without touching the backend).
+
+The reason this matters now is that the RaBitQ compression number had landed and held. 21× compression, 100% recall@10, 957 QPS single-thread, 3.13× over exact flat. Compress once in the cache, serve from any backend — that is the leverage that makes the cache-first stance coherent. The bundle sidecar (`table.rulake.json`, 300 bytes, `RuLakeBundle` at `src/bundle.rs:113`) is the portable unit that carries that compression's entropy across processes: `(data_ref, dim, rotation_seed, rerank_factor, generation, rvf_witness, pii_policy, lineage_id, memory_class)` with a SHAKE-256 witness over the witness-affecting fields. Two ruLake instances reading the same bundle from different backends share the cache pointer because the witness is the cache-key anchor — the cross-backend dedup story.
+
+What ADR-155 is *not* doing is also load-bearing. It is not building a vector database (ruLake does not own storage; customers who want a managed vector DB stay on Pinecone / Weaviate / Milvus / LanceDB). It is not replacing BigQuery or Snowflake (these are backends to ruLake, not competitors). It is not promising sub-millisecond latency (cache-hit targets ≤2 ms p99; federated cold path is network-bound). It is not GDPR-compliant out of the box (v1 supports phase-1 logical delete with 30-day phase-2 backend delete; crypto-shredding is v2). It is not a SQL dialect (queries are structured RVF wire — vector + filter predicates — not ANSI SQL; we do not try to reimplement Trino).
+
+## The decision in detail
+
+What got picked. **The `BackendAdapter` trait is the contract** (`src/backend.rs:110`-area). Four required methods (`id`, `list_collections`, `pull_vectors`, `generation`) plus an optional `current_bundle` override. Adapters ship as sub-crates so customers with proprietary lakes can implement their own without forking ruLake. **The cache is RaBitQ-native.** Pulled vectors are immediately compressed into 1-bit RaBitQ codes via `ruvector_rabitq::RabitqPlusIndex`. Hot queries answer from the cache (957 QPS, 21× compression); cold queries pull from the backend and prime the cache. Cache coherence rides per-backend manifest-generation numbers. **Governance is a single choke point** between the wire and the planner — RBAC, column masking, lineage, GDPR phase-1 delete, PII classification, and audit log all live there, so adding a backend does not multiply governance work. **BigQuery-native compute is a push-down optimisation inside the `BigQueryBackend`**, not a new product shape. **RVF is the lingua franca both upstream and downstream.** **The portable unit is the bundle sidecar `table.rulake.json`, not the UDF.** The `Generation` is an opaque union (`Num(u64)` for mtimes/versions; `Opaque(String)` for UUIDs and hashes) so Iceberg/Snowflake tokens fit (`src/bundle.rs:54`-`98`).
+
+What got rejected. Plug-in-per-lake (the first cut) — multiplies per-backend work and leaves cross-backend federation users without a story. Standalone vector-DB with Parquet import — competes head-on with Pinecone/Weaviate/Milvus/LanceDB without a clear 10× moat. Pure Iceberg table-format extension — real but slow (Iceberg spec evolution is measured in years; kept as a v2 contribution upstream). Trino/Presto connector — Trino assumes SQL and a vector ANN query does not fit the SQL shape cleanly without UDFs; kept as v2. JVM intermediary in Java/Scala — duplicates the hot path in a slower runtime. RVF-purely-inside-a-customer-notebook — works for single-machine single-user; the intermediary unlocks multi-user, multi-backend, governance. Push-through-only (no cache) — simpler coherence but throughput gated by the slowest backend; kept as a mode flag for customers who cannot tolerate cache staleness.
+
+The load-bearing trade-offs. **Cache coherence is a real problem.** Backend updates do not notify ruLake by default. Mitigations are per-backend: Parquet uses file-mtime + filename hash; Iceberg uses snapshot id on the table manifest; BigQuery uses `INFORMATION_SCHEMA.TABLE_STORAGE.last_modified_time`; Delta uses `_delta_log` transaction version; Snowflake uses `SYSTEM$CLUSTERING_INFORMATION` / change streams. Customers with strict consistency requirements run in push-through-only mode and accept the QPS hit. **Latency hop.** Even on cache-hit the RVF-wire round trip adds 1–5 ms over direct library use; customers who call RaBitQ in-process stay in-process. **Owns a new surface** — the planner, the router, the cache eviction policy, the per-backend coherence protocols. **BigQuery's own native Vector Search competes** for pure-BQ customers; ruLake's value is cross-backend + governance + RaBitQ determinism, not single-backend feature parity. **The crate-shape discipline** is preserved by ADR-001: no root workspace, sibling crates only, no `*.workspace = true`, so the IPFS dependencies do not bleed into every consumer of `rulake` core.
+
+## Capabilities
+
+**Federated search across heterogeneous backends.** `RuLake::search_federated(targets, query, k)` (`src/lake.rs:521`) is a rayon parallel walk over `(backend, collection)` pairs with adaptive per-shard rerank floored at 5 (`src/lake.rs:504`, `:533`). Pre-adaptive, a K-shard federated search paid K× the rerank cost; post-adaptive, the total pre-merge rerank budget is roughly constant in K. Measured: 4-shard concurrent federation went from 0.60× → 0.98× the single-shard QPS, with recall@10 ≥ 0.85 on clustered D=128 n=5k (test `adaptive_per_shard_rerank_preserves_recall`). The over-request formula `k' = k + ceil(sqrt(k * ln(S)))` (`src/lake.rs:584`-`590`) closes the data-skew gap that Weaviate / Elasticsearch leave with naive k-per-shard fan-out — at k=10 S=4 this gives k'=13, at k=10 S=16 k'=16.
+
+**Cross-backend cache dedup via witness.** Two backends with identical underlying data produce the same `rvf_witness` because the witness recipe is purely a function of `(data_ref, dim, rotation_seed, rerank_factor, generation_with_variant_tag)`. `src/cache.rs::install_prebuilt_interned` shares the compressed entry. Test `two_backends_share_cache_when_witness_matches` is the gate.
+
+**Bundle publish + refresh** as the cache sidecar protocol. `RuLake::publish_bundle(key, dir)` writes `table.rulake.json` to a directory atomically (temp + rename); `RuLake::refresh_from_bundle_dir(key, dir)` reads it back and returns `RefreshResult::{UpToDate, Invalidated, BundleMissing}`. A corrupt or tampered sidecar surfaces as `InvalidParameter` via the witness-verification path so a poisoned publish cannot silently invalidate the cache. The sidecar daemon itself is ~10 lines of user code — the protocol is the primitive, not a daemon.
+
+**Warm restart via persisted index.** `RuLake::save_cache_to_dir(key, dir)` snapshots a primed cache entry to `dir/index.rbpx` plus the companion sidecar; `RuLake::warm_from_dir(key, dir)` reads them back, witness-verifies the sidecar, dim/rerank-checks the index, and installs into the cache via the interned path — no backend round-trip, no RaBitQ recompression. Two operators warming the same bundle share one compressed entry.
+
+**Worked example.** A research collaboration has 100k vectors in a Parquet file on GCS, 10k vectors in an in-memory `LocalBackend` for tests, and 50k vectors served by an `FsBackend` on a local SSD. The operator registers all three backends under one `RuLake` instance. A federated query (`search_federated([("gcs", "papers"), ("local", "fixtures"), ("fs", "snapshot")], q, 10)`) fans out in parallel: each shard primes its cache on miss, each shard's RaBitQ search runs concurrently, the top-13 per shard (over-requested for 3-shard skew) merges by score, the global top-10 returns. Per-backend `cache_stats_by_backend()` reports hit rate, prime time, and witness count per backend; per-collection `cache_stats_by_collection()` is one level finer. The 95% hit-rate gate is measurable directly from `CacheStats::hit_rate()` without external tracing.
+
+## Trust & correctness contract
+
+The trust anchor is the per-bundle witness as cache key. Five things matter, all citable.
+
+**The witness recipe is bytes-only.** `compute_witness` (`src/bundle.rs:362`-area) is SHAKE-256(32) over a domain-prefixed, length-prefixed, variant-tagged concatenation of `(data_ref, dim, rotation_seed, rerank_factor, generation)`. The domain prefix `rulake-bundle-witness-v1|` makes the digest non-overlapping with any other SHAKE consumer; every variable-length input is preceded by its length in little-endian u64 so `"a|b"` and `"ab|"` cannot collide; the `Generation` enum carries a 1-byte variant tag at `src/bundle.rs:73`-`98` (`0x00` for `Num`, `0x01` for `Opaque`) so `Num(7)` and `Opaque("\x07\0\0\0\0\0\0\0")` cannot produce the same digest — a real bug closed by the 2026-04-23 security audit, bumped as `format_version = 2` at `src/bundle.rs:163`.
+
+**The witness is the cache-key anchor.** `src/cache.rs::install_prebuilt_interned` keys by witness; `RuLake::cache_witness_of(key)` (`src/lake.rs:144`) returns the witness pinned by a `(backend, collection)` pointer; `RuLake::cache_refcount_of(witness)` (`src/lake.rs:157`) counts the external pointers resolving to it; `RuLake::cache_entry_count()` (`src/lake.rs:151`) is smaller than the pointer count when multiple backends share a witness.
+
+**The bundle is verified at every parse.** `RuLakeBundle::from_json` (`src/bundle.rs:215`) enforces `MAX_JSON_BYTES = 64 * 1024`, per-field length caps, and rejects `format_version > 2`. `RuLakeBundle::verify_witness` (`src/bundle.rs:191`) recomputes the digest from the parsed fields and returns the equality. `RuLake::refresh_from_bundle_dir` rejects a tampered sidecar with `InvalidParameter` so a corrupt publish does not silently poison the cache (`src/lake.rs:228`-`258`).
+
+**Construction is the only witness-emitting path.** `RuLakeBundle::new` (`src/bundle.rs:166`) computes the witness during construction; the bundle is never observed without its anchor. Backends that synthesise bundles in `current_bundle` always go through `RuLakeBundle::new` (the `IpfsBackend::current_bundle` fast path at `ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:354` is the canonical example).
+
+**The variant-tag fix and the format-version bump are explicit.** `RuLakeBundle::FORMAT_VERSION = 2` at `src/bundle.rs:163` is the on-disk version; v1 bundles will not match v2 witnesses, which is intended — the variant-tag fix is a breaking change to the witness, and operators with persisted v1 bundles re-prime the cache. The doc comment at `src/bundle.rs:158`-`162` is the canonical record.
+
+## Reference implementation status
+
+What shipped, in the order it landed.
+
+- **M1 (weeks 1–2, shipped, measured)** — the `rulake` crate scaffold with `BackendAdapter` trait, `LocalBackend` + `FsBackend`, RaBitQ-cache glue, witness-addressed cache with cross-backend sharing, LRU eviction over unpinned entries, rayon parallel fan-out with adaptive per-shard rerank, bundle sidecar protocol, and hit-rate / prime-time instrumentation. 28 tests passing. Acceptance numbers from `BENCHMARK.md`: 1.02× intermediary tax on `LocalBackend` at n=100k, 2-shard 1.97× / 4-shard 3.86× rayon prime-time speedups, recall@10 > 90% on clustered D=128 rerank×20 single-shard, Send+Sync under contention (8 threads × 50 queries).
+- **M1.5 (acceptance test for the cache-first reframe)** — `CacheStats::hit_rate()` (`Option<f64>`) and `avg_prime_ms()` plus raw `total_prime_ms` / `last_prime_ms` exposed. +- **M2 storage adapters (shipped)** — `gcs-backend/` (Parquet on GCS via `object_store`, commit `c706dc6`), `ipfs-backend/` v0.1 (witness-anchored bundle distribution by CIDv1, with R-IPFS-1 hard-refuse on CID mismatch in commit `56b497b`).

+- **Crate published to crates.io** as `rulake@2.2.0` (commit `f04eb95`); manifest at `Cargo.toml:1`-`36`.
+
+What stayed roadmapped at M4+. Iceberg / Delta / BigQuery adapters. RBAC / PII / lineage / audit choke point (M4 in the original plan). Crypto-shredding for GDPR (v2). JVM client for the wire protocol (enterprise customer ask, deferred). A Trino/Presto connector. Push-down negotiation policy (when does the planner prefer native push-down over the cache? +
+## Composition with the rest of ruLake
+
+The crate is the trunk that every other ruLake artefact branches from.
+
+The `BackendAdapter` trait at `src/backend.rs:110` is what every backend implements — `LocalBackend` (in-tree), `FsBackend` (in-tree, the `ruvec1` binary format at `src/fs_backend.rs`), `GcsParquetBackend` (sibling crate `gcs-backend/`), `IpfsBackend` (sibling crate `ipfs-backend/`), `RvdnaT0Backend` (sibling crate `rvdna-backend/`, ADR-007), `RuquStateVectorBackend` (sibling crate `ruqu-backend/`, ADR-008). Each is `cargo install`-able independently and registered in the operator's serving binary via `Arc<dyn BackendAdapter>`.
+
+The bundle (`src/bundle.rs:113`) is what travels between processes. Its witness anchors the cache (`src/cache.rs`); its `data_ref` points at the underlying byte stream (a backend's URI); its `generation` is the coherence token. The witness is what makes cross-deployment cache sharing safe; the `generation` is what makes coherence checkable; the `lineage_id` is what makes the audit pipeline join across federation hops.
+
+The lake (`src/lake.rs:48`) is the public entry point. It owns the backend registry, the cache, and the consistency posture. `search_one` (`src/lake.rs:475`) is the single-target path; `search_federated` (`src/lake.rs:521`) is the multi-target path; `publish_bundle` / `refresh_from_bundle_dir` is the sidecar protocol; `save_cache_to_dir` / `warm_from_dir` is the warm-restart path; `cache_stats_by_backend` / `cache_stats_by_collection` is the operator-visible KPI surface (`src/lake.rs:127`, `:136`).

+The MCP server (`mcp-server/`, ADR-004) wraps the lake's surface in eight tools (3 Read tier, 2 Publish, 3 Admin) and three resources (`rulake://stats`, `rulake://bundle/{backend}/{collection}`, `rulake://audit/tail`). +
+A substrate, in this framing, is anything that satisfies the trait and produces witnesses through the canonical recipe. ADR-155 is the contract that makes substrate-shaped contributions land without a federation rewrite, a cache-layer fork, or an audit-pipeline duplication.

+## Open questions
+
+Push-down negotiation is the largest M2+ unknown — when a backend supports native push-down (BQ Vector Search, Snowflake Cortex), at what point does the planner prefer it over the cache? Cost accounting — when the planner pushes down to BQ, whose BQ credits are burned? Needs a customer-facing cost-attribution story. Remote-backend tax measurement — `BENCHMARK.md`'s 1.02× tax on `LocalBackend` is the floor; M2 needs to measure the real tax on a Parquet-on-GCS prime and document the p50/p99 numbers the BQ UDF path can expect. Strategic positioning unknowns — invisible-infrastructure-vs-user-facing-query-layer, strict-freshness-vs-10×-throughput as a product knob. The ADR is honest about each as deliberately not "engineering open questions" but answers that shape what ruLake *is*.

+# ipfs-backend — A Deep Introduction
+
+## TL;DR
+
+`ipfs-backend/` is a sibling crate that implements ruLake's `BackendAdapter` trait against IPFS, but stores only the `table.rulake.json` bundle — the small, witness-anchored sidecar — and never the vector bodies, which stay on whichever backend already serves them. It runs in three modes (kubo HTTP RPC, gateway-only, or kubo with gateway fallback), maps the resolved CID into `Generation::Opaque(String)` so the existing witness recipe is unchanged, and treats `data_ref ≠ "ipfs://{cid}"` as a hard refusal (`IPFS_BUNDLE_CID_MISMATCH`) after the focused security review surfaced R-IPFS-1 as a CID-substitution vector. The result is a content-addressed, network-public distribution plane for the cache anchor itself, sized for the bundle's 64 KiB cap and the single-block fetches that come with it.
+
+## Introduction
+
+ruLake's bundle is small and load-bearing in equal measure. A `table.rulake.json` carries `(data_ref, dim, rotation_seed, rerank_factor, generation, rvf_witness, pii_policy, lineage_id, memory_class)` and is bounded by `MAX_JSON_BYTES = 64 * 1024` at `src/bundle.rs:218` — a few hundred bytes in practice, with two orders of magnitude of headroom. Because the witness is a SHAKE-256(32) digest over the four content-shaping fields plus the variant-tagged `Generation` (`src/bundle.rs:362`-area, `src/bundle.rs:73`-area for the variant-tag fix from the 2026-04-23 audit), two ruLake instances independently constructing the same bundle produce the same witness without ever comparing payloads. That property is what makes cross-deployment cache sharing safe in the first place — the bundle, not the bytes, is what travels.
+
+What was missing before ADR-005 was a *transport* for the bundle that matched its content-addressed shape. The first cloud backend, `gcs-backend/` (commit `c706dc6`), addressed Parquet objects by `gs://bucket/key` URIs and per-object generation tokens. That works for one cloud and one tenant, but it carries an authentication graph — IAM roles, signed URLs, bucket-level policies — that has to be settled before two parties can share a cache anchor. IPFS is the natural fit for the opposite property: you do not need to know who pinned a CID to fetch it. If the CID resolves, the bytes are exactly what they were when the witness was computed, because the CID *is* a hash of the bytes. The naming axis (where the bundle lives, who fetches it, through which gateway) becomes orthogonal to the trust axis (whether the bundle's witness verifies against its own fields).
+
+The historical context is that several adjacent decisions had to land first. The witness had to be cryptographically sound — that landed with the `Generation` variant tag in `src/bundle.rs:73`-area, closing a collision between `Num(7)` and `Opaque("\x07\0\0\0\0\0\0\0")` that the security audit caught. The MCP server's `rulake://bundle/{backend}/{collection}` resource contract had to commit to a cheap `current_bundle()` override (ADR-004), because IPFS body fetches over a public gateway can be hundreds of milliseconds and a DAG walk would push that into seconds. The crate-layout discipline from ADR-001 — sibling crates, no root workspace, no `*.workspace = true` — had to be in place so the IPFS dependencies (`reqwest`, `cid`, `multibase`) did not bleed into every consumer of `rulake` core. By the time ADR-005 opened, the question was no longer "is content-addressed distribution a good idea" but "what is the smallest crate that picks kubo as default, falls back to public gateways, and reuses the bundle's existing trust property without inventing a second one."
+
+The reason this matters *now*, rather than at some abstract later, is operational. The agent-developer persona that ADR-006 surfaces wants to publish a bundle from a browser tab, paste a CID into a peer's tab, and have that peer's `verifyBundleJson` re-derive the same SHAKE-256 against the bytes the gateway delivered. There is no other distribution plane in the ruLake stack that lets that flow happen without an HTTP server, a bucket policy, or a CDN configuration. IPFS is the one — but only if the backend honors the witness/CID binding strictly, which is the load-bearing decision the focused security review forced.
+
+## The decision in detail
+
+The crate ships as `ruvector-rulake-ipfs` v0.1.0 at `ipfs-backend/`, sibling to `gcs-backend/`. Three operating modes, picked by the operator at construction time (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:39`-area):
+
+- `Mode::Kubo` — talks to a kubo HTTP RPC (default `http://127.0.0.1:5001`), reads via `POST /api/v0/cat`, writes via `POST /api/v0/add` followed by `POST /api/v0/pin/add`. Optional bearer token threaded through `Mode::Kubo { api_token }` for kubo nodes behind a Caddy/nginx auth layer.
+- `Mode::Gateway` — read-only, against a public IPFS Foundation gateway (`https://ipfs.io` by default; `dweb.link`, `w3s.link` documented as alternatives; Cloudflare's `cloudflare-ipfs.com` deliberately excluded because it was decommissioned 2024-08). `publish_bundle` errors at the trait boundary with `RuLakeError::Backend` so a caller in this mode cannot accidentally write through a gateway that does not accept writes anyway.
+- `Mode::KuboWithGatewayFallback` — kubo first; on a kubo `cat` failure, fall back to the gateway with a `tracing::warn!` line carrying `cid` and `error` so the degradation is operator-visible (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:225`-area).
+
+Two notable implementation choices defended by the ADR. First, the dependency on `ipfs-api-backend-hyper` was *removed* before v0.1 shipped — it transitively pulled `multihash 0.17` → `core2 0.4` (yanked), and the kubo HTTP RPC is small enough (`add`, `cat`, `pin/add`) that talking to it via `reqwest` directly is ~50 LOC of multipart upload and JSON parse (`ipfs-backend/Cargo.toml` header comment). Second, the backend's `pull_vectors` deliberately errors (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:331`): IPFS is the bundle distribution plane, not the vector body plane. Operators wire IPFS for the witness round-trip and a sister backend (`gcs`, `fs`, `local`) for the bytes. ADR-155's cache-first stance says backends are interchangeable so long as their generation tokens are reliable; IPFS's role here is to carry the generation token cheaply, not to serve a 50 MB Parquet over a public gateway.
+
+What got rejected. Embedded full-node Rust IPFS (`rust-ipfs`, alpha; `iroh`, IPFS-compat layer not shipped) — both pull a libp2p stack, DHT routing tables, NAT traversal state, and peer-id key material into the serving binary for no upside when the operator already has a kubo daemon. Vector-bodies-on-IPFS — technically possible via UnixFS, but a 100k×128 f32 collection is ~50 MB, which is `~10–60 s` on a public gateway cold-fetch and breaks the 1.02× tax envelope from `BENCHMARK.md`. The MCP server's `transfer_ipfs-resolve` tool — that resolves IPNS names to CIDs and is fine as a config-time helper, but using it in the read path means every bundle fetch goes through a public resolver with no auth and no SLA.
+
+The load-bearing trade-off is the witness↔CID binding. The bundle's `rvf_witness` is SHAKE-256; the CID is sha2-256 multihash; they are different hash functions and cannot be made equal. The mapping the ADR commits to is: `data_ref` becomes `ipfs://<cid>`, `generation` becomes `Generation::Opaque(<cid>)`, and the witness is recomputed through `RuLakeBundle::new` so it reflects the IPFS-side fields. R-IPFS-1, the security finding from the focused review, was that an earlier `fetch_bundle` was warn-only on `data_ref ≠ ipfs://{cid}` — a CID-substitution attacker could re-pin a legitimately-witnessed bundle under a different CID and serve it back to a caller that recorded the original. The fix in commit `56b497b` is a hard refuse with code `IPFS_BUNDLE_CID_MISMATCH` (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:284`-`303`).
+
+## Capabilities
+
+**Bundle-publishing.** An operator computes a bundle locally, calls `IpfsBackend::publish_bundle(collection, &bundle)` (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:260`), and gets back a CIDv1. The flow is `bundle.to_json()` → `kubo_add` (multipart POST with `cid-version=1`) → `kubo_pin_add`. The CID is recorded against the collection through `record_cid` so subsequent `fetch_bundle`/`current_bundle` calls resolve it without operator re-entry. Republishing identical bytes returns the same CID by construction — content addressing means the network does the dedup.
+
+**Cross-deployment cache priming.** Two ruLake instances on two hosts both register the same `(collection, cid)` mapping (or one publishes and shares the CID with the other). Each instance's `current_bundle` resolves the CID through its configured mode, parses the JSON, verifies that the bundle's `data_ref` matches `ipfs://<cid>` (refusing on mismatch), and constructs a re-anchored `RuLakeBundle` through `RuLakeBundle::new`. Because the witness recipe is purely a function of bytes, both instances derive the same `rvf_witness` and share the cache pointer through `src/cache.rs::install_prebuilt_interned`. No bucket policy, no signed URL, no shared IAM — just a CID and a witness that happens to verify.
+
+**Gateway-only sandbox / CI.** A CI job that needs to pull a published bundle for verification but cannot run a kubo daemon constructs `IpfsBackend::gateway_only(id, "https://ipfs.io")`. The mode rejects `publish_bundle` at the trait boundary, so a runaway test cannot accidentally try to write through a gateway. Reads go straight to `gateway_cat` (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:483`).
+
+**Worked example.** Imagine a research collaboration where two universities both compute a vector index over a shared corpus of ~50k documents stored as Parquet on each institution's own GCS bucket. Each institution's ruLake registers a `GcsParquetBackend` for the bytes. One institution computes the bundle, pins it on its kubo daemon (`publish_bundle`), and shares the CIDv1 with the other. The peer registers an `IpfsBackend` in `Mode::KuboWithGatewayFallback`, registers the `(collection, cid)` mapping, and calls `RuLake::current_bundle(&key)`. The IPFS backend fetches the bundle (kubo first, gateway on miss), refuses if the bundle's `data_ref` does not match the CID, and returns a re-anchored bundle whose witness verifies. The peer's cache shares the witness with the publisher's — both `cache_witness_of(&key)` calls return the same hex digest — and either institution's subsequent `search_one` against that key serves out of the same RaBitQ-compressed entry. The bytes never crossed institutional boundaries; only the 300-byte sidecar did.
+
+## Trust & correctness contract
+
+The trust anchor is the CID↔witness binding, enforced at fetch time. The relevant section is `IpfsBackend::fetch_bundle` at `ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:271`-`306`:
+
+1. Resolve `(collection) → CID` from operator config (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:154`).
+2. `cat` the CID through whichever client the mode allows (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:215`-`239`).
+3. Parse the bytes as UTF-8, then through `RuLakeBundle::from_json` (`src/bundle.rs:215`), which enforces the 64 KiB cap and per-field length caps and rejects `format_version > 2`.
+4. **Hard refuse** if `bundle.data_ref != "ipfs://{cid}"` — emit a `tracing::error!` with `got` and `expected`, return `RuLakeError::Backend` with the `IPFS_BUNDLE_CID_MISMATCH` code embedded in the detail string (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:292`-`303`).
+5. Return the verified bundle.
+
+Step 4 is the load-bearing one. Witness equality alone does not catch a CID-substitution attack: an attacker who controls a pinning service can re-publish a legitimately-witnessed bundle under a different CID, and a caller that had previously recorded the original CID would silently serve from the new one. The `data_ref ↔ CID` binding is the only check that catches that — if `data_ref` is `ipfs://bafkrei…ABC` but the fetched CID is `bafkrei…XYZ`, the operator is asserting two different identities for the same bytes, and the backend refuses.
+
+The five operator-visible audit codes are the surface of this contract: `IPFS_OK` (read succeeded), `IPFS_HTTP_ERROR` (network/HTTP failure), `IPFS_BUNDLE_VERIFIED` (`from_json` accepted and witness verified), `IPFS_FETCH_FAILED` (round-trip failed end-to-end), `IPFS_BUNDLE_CID_MISMATCH` (the R-IPFS-1 refusal). The Console emits the same codes from its WASM-local IPFS path (`ui/src/components/screens.jsx:1137`, `:1168`, `:1228`, `:1775`, `:1784`) so the browser's audit ring and the server's structured logs share a vocabulary.
+
+The fast-path optimisation in `current_bundle` (`ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:354`-`372`) is worth calling out: when the operator pre-set both `cid` and `dim` at register time, the backend synthesises the bundle locally through `RuLakeBundle::new` without any IPFS round-trip, which honours the ADR-004 resource contract's "cheap `current_bundle()`" requirement at zero network cost. The slow path falls back to `fetch_bundle` and pays one single-block `cat` — bundles are bounded by the JSON cap to a single IPFS block (default 256 KiB), so no DAG walk is involved.
+

+
+What shipped, with paths.

+
+What did *not* ship in v0.1, deliberately. The deployment annex (ADR-005 §9–§11) for the single-node kubo on `ruv-dev` GCP — documented, not provisioned. The `pinning-service` Cargo feature for Pinata / Storacha / Filebase — gated off by default to keep the v0.1 surface narrow. The encryption envelope for confidential bundles — documented in ADR-005 §8 as an `examples/` exhibit because operators will ask, but not part of the backend itself. IPNS resolution in the read path — the operator resolves IPNS once at config time; the backend reads CIDs.
+
+## Composition with the rest of ruLake
+
+`ipfs-backend/` slots into the `BackendAdapter` graph the same way `local`, `fs`, and `gcs` do. From the lake's perspective it is a source of `current_bundle` answers; the extra contract — that the IPFS layer carries the witness sidecar and *not* the vectors — is encoded by `pull_vectors` errors with a structured message pointing the operator at the body-store backend they should pair it with.
+
+Three concrete composition points. The same SHAKE-256 digest that anchors a Parquet table or a Local fixture anchors the IPFS-resolved bundle, which is what makes cross-backend cache sharing through `src/cache.rs::install_prebuilt_interned` correct without a witness-alias table.
+
+Second, the federation primitive. `RuLake::search_federated` (`src/lake.rs:521`) is a rayon parallel walk over `(backend, collection)` pairs. What changes is the `Consistency` posture an operator should pick: ADR-155's `Consistency::Eventual { ttl_ms }` is the recommended default for IPFS-served collections because `Fresh` triggers a generation check on every search, and on IPFS the generation check is a network call (or a stable_hash over the CID, in `IpfsBackend::generation` at `ipfs-backend/src/backend.rs:341`).

Bundle-only mode (vector bodies stay in the body-store backend). Three modes: kubo / gateway-only / kubo + gateway-fallback."

+# ruLake as Memory Substrate — A Deep Introduction
+
+## TL;DR
+
+ADR-156 is positioning, not implementation: it ratifies that ruLake — a vector execution cache with deterministic compression, witness-addressed sharing, federated refill, and a bundle protocol — is the right *memory hierarchy* for an agent brain system to sit on top of, and that ruLake should stay as substrate rather than absorb the brain. The contract is the substrate acceptance test — an agent can recall, verify, forget, and rehydrate memory across hot/warm/cold tiers without knowing where the memory physically lives — and five of the six guarantees were already shipped under ADR-155 M1 (the sixth, "compact", explicitly belongs to a higher layer like RVM/Cognitum). The only additions ADR-156 *requires* are nomenclature and a small `Consistency::Frozen` knob; everything load-bearing was already in the box, which is why the ADR is "Accepted (positioning ratified)" rather than a build commitment.
+
+## Introduction
+
+ADR-155 had built ruLake as a cache-first vector execution fabric: a RaBitQ-compressed cache with witness-addressed sharing, federated refill, and a bundle protocol for warehouse-driven coherence. The strategic review on 2026-04-22 asked a different question. The measured properties — ~1.02× overhead on the cache-hit path versus direct RaBitQ; witness-authenticated freshness with three modes (Fresh / Eventual / Frozen); deterministic compression so two processes reading the same bytes produce byte-identical codes; content-addressed cache sharing; atomic sidecar publish + refresh — are exactly the properties an agent brain memory hierarchy needs from its storage layer. The question ADR-156 answers is whether ruLake should grow *into* that role or stay as substrate underneath it.
+
+The historical context is that "agent memory" had been a hand-wavy phrase. LangChain's memory abstractions, the various RAG stacks, the chat-history-as-vectors patterns — all of them had a shape: store some embeddings somewhere, look them up by similarity, hope the model uses them well. None of them carried the *substrate* property — the property where the storage layer makes verifiable claims about what was retrieved, where two agents on two machines querying the same data get the same byte-for-byte answer, where staleness is detectable and refusable rather than silently served. ruLake's witness-anchored cache is exactly that property, just framed for warehouses. The reframe ADR-156 records is that the same property serves the agent-memory use case without modification — it just needed a name.
+
+The reason the substrate framing matters now, rather than later, is structural. The agent-developer persona that ADR-006 surfaced (the in-browser Console, the WASM-local mode, the App Store screen) is the same persona that consumes the substrate framing. They are not reading `docs/adrs/ADR-155-rulake-datalake-layer.md` and translating "vector execution cache with deterministic compression and federated refill" into agent terms; they are reading the README's "Why agents in particular" section and the App Store screen at `ui/src/components/screens.jsx:2018`-area and seeing the substrate framing first. ADR-156 is the canonical record that the framing is correct — that ruLake is not pretending to be something it is not when it presents itself this way.
+
+The harder, less obvious decision in ADR-156 is what *not* to build. Three rejected paths: absorb the brain into ruLake (violates the substrate separation; a cache-first execution fabric does not know what "episodic" means, and if it does, it has stopped being a substrate); build a new sibling crate `rulake-memory` (premature — nothing in M1 needs it; the small additions inside ruLake are load-bearing, anything bigger waits for a brain-system consumer); fork into two products `ruLake` and `ruMem` (the measured properties are identical; the split would double maintenance for no technical win). The decision recorded is that ruLake stays as substrate, the brain consumer is hypothetical until it is not, and the additions are nomenclature plus one freshness mode.
+
+What the ADR is *not* doing is also load-bearing. It is not committing to build the brain system inside ruLake. It is not adding a memory-classification API. It is not adding a recall-policy framework. It is not adding contradiction scoring or trust-graph edges. Those are cognitive-layer concerns and they belong in RVM / Cognitum, not in the cache. The ADR is recording what ruLake *is also good for*, with one explicit acceptance test, and stopping there.
+
+## The decision in detail
+
+What got picked. **ruLake stays as substrate.** The brain consumer (hypothetical) owns memory-type semantics (episodic / semantic / procedural / identity / policy / observation — cognitive labels that the substrate stores as opaque strings if asked but never interprets), recall policy (which candidates matter, how to combine vector similarity with graph neighborhood / recency / trust / contradiction / mincut boundary), and mutation policy (when to write, merge, delete, compact, rehydrate). ruLake owns the primitives — `VectorCache` + RaBitQ codes for hot memory, `BackendAdapter::pull_vectors` + `RuLakeBundle` for warm memory, the backend-adapter contract for cold memory (Parquet/GCS/BQ/Iceberg), `Consistency::{Fresh, Eventual, Frozen}` for the freshness contract, the SHAKE-256 witness for witnessed state, the sidecar protocol for cross-process handoff, `CacheStats::{hit_rate, avg_prime_ms, last_prime_ms}` for observability, LRU cap over unpinned entries for multi-tier eviction.
+
+**`Consistency::Frozen` (shipped 2026-04-22).** A new mode that lets the caller assert the bundle's witness is immutable for the cache's lifetime. Maps to "Frozen for audit" from the strategic review's three-mode knob. Explicit `refresh_from_bundle_dir` still works; only the *automatic* coherence check is suppressed. This is the smallest addition that opens the audit market — compliance-heavy customers who cannot tolerate automatic coherence drift on historical snapshots get a serving mode that says "I will not check; if you want me to, ask me explicitly."
+
+**Memory-class tag on the bundle (shipped at `src/bundle.rs:143`).** A `memory_class: Option<String>` field — caller-defined, opaque, surfaces through stats but never interpreted. Agent systems tag bundles with cognitive labels like `"episodic"` / `"semantic"` / `"procedural"` / `"identity"`; ruLake stores the string and returns it. The field is `serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")` so old bundles without the tag deserialise unchanged, and crucially the tag is *not* part of the witness — changing the memory-class on the same vectors does not invalidate a cache entry. Two bundles with identical data but different classes share the cache. The doc comment at `src/bundle.rs:143`-`154` is the canonical record: "Opaque by design — brain systems own the semantics, substrate owns the persistence."
+
+**Explicit separation of read / write / compact paths (doc, not code).** ruLake v1 is read-optimized and append-only; writes go through RVF ingest, not through ruLake. The ADR records that split as load-bearing — compaction belongs to RVM / Cognitum, not to ruLake. This is the load-bearing decision because it is what keeps ruLake from drifting into "brain system" scope; the substrate's job is to be a fast, honest cache, not to schedule compaction or merge episodic chains.
+
+What got rejected. Absorbing the brain — violates substrate separation; a cache that knows what "episodic" means has stopped being a cache. A new sibling crate `rulake-memory` — premature; the tag plus Frozen additions are small enough to live inside ruLake without scope creep. Forking into two products — measured properties are identical; the positioning serves both. Per-memory-class stats (`CacheStats::by_class` as a `HashMap<String, CacheStats>`) — flagged as scope-creep toward brain features; deferred until a consumer asks.
+
+The load-bearing trade-offs. **Adds a third framing.** ADR-155 v2 said "intermediary", v3 said "cache-first fabric", ADR-156 says "memory substrate." Risk that customers conflate them. The mitigation is to keep the one-liner in ADR-155 as the authoritative crate description and treat ADR-156 as the *consumer positioning* for agent systems. **"Forget" is ambiguous.** Substrate-level forget (cache pointer drop) is not cryptographic forget. Customers with strict GDPR needs must pair substrate invalidation with RVF crypto-shredding. This is already the ADR-155 position but worth restating. **Tag-on-bundle is a schema change.** Adding `memory_class: Option<String>` is a backwards-compatible field addition but still needs a `format_version` decision; the choice was not to bump (the field is `serde(default, skip_serializing_if)`).
+
+## Capabilities
+
+**Recall.** `RuLake::search_one(backend, collection, query, k)` (`src/lake.rs:475`) and `RuLake::search_federated(targets, query, k)` (`src/lake.rs:521`) both return `SearchResult { backend, collection, id, score }`. The result carries the backend and collection so an agent can audit which substrate produced the hit; it does not carry the `data_ref` so the agent never has to know whether the vectors lived in GCS, in IPFS-pinned bundles, on local disk, or in a `LocalBackend` fixture.
+
+**Verify.** `RuLakeBundle::verify_witness` (`src/bundle.rs:191`) recomputes the SHAKE-256 from the parsed fields and returns the equality. `RuLake::current_bundle(key)` (`src/lake.rs:179`) returns the bundle for any registered `(backend, collection)` so an agent can verify a result's provenance against the cache's current witness.
+
+**Forget.** `RuLake::invalidate_cache(key)` (`src/lake.rs:164`) drops the cache pointer for a `(backend, collection)` pair; if it was the last pointer at the witness, the underlying compressed entry is garbage-collected. Combined with `Consistency::Fresh`, the next search re-primes against the backend; combined with `Consistency::Frozen`, the next search refuses (because the cache is empty and the consistency mode says "do not auto-prime"). "Forget" in the substrate sense is the cache pointer drop; "forget" in the cryptographic-shred sense stays as the ADR-155 GDPR follow-up.
+
+**Compact.** Out of scope. Belongs to RVM / Cognitum. The ADR is explicit about this: a substrate that compacts is owning a write/merge path that the brain layer needs for cognitive-coherence reasons, and pushing those reasons into the substrate would muddle the boundary. ruLake's ingest is RVF; ruLake's compaction is whatever RVM ships.
+
+**Rehydrate.** `RuLake::warm_from_dir(key, dir)` (`src/lake.rs:408`) reads `dir/index.rbpx` plus the companion `table.rulake.json`, witness-verifies the sidecar, dim/rerank-checks the index, and installs into the cache via `install_prebuilt_interned`. No backend round-trip, no RaBitQ recompression — the primed entry pops back into reality in O(file-read) time. Two operators warming the same bundle share one compressed entry.
+
+**Location-transparency.** `SearchResult` carries `backend` + `collection`; the agent never touches `data_ref`. The substrate's location-transparency claim is operationally enforced by the trait — `BackendAdapter::pull_vectors` is called by the cache, not by the caller, and the caller's interface is `(backend, collection, query, k) → results` regardless of whether the backend is local memory, an `FsBackend` over `ruvec1` files, a Parquet object on GCS, an IPFS-pinned bundle's referenced bytes, or a federation across all four.
+
+**Worked example.** An agent owns a memory hierarchy with three tiers: hot (recent conversational embeddings, in-RAM, `Consistency::Fresh`), warm (the user's pinned documents, `FsBackend` on local disk, `Consistency::Eventual { ttl_ms: 60_000 }`), cold (the user's archived corpus, GCS-backed Parquet, `Consistency::Eventual { ttl_ms: 600_000 }`). The agent registers three backends in one ruLake instance, tags each backend's bundles with `memory_class = "episodic"` / `"semantic"` / `"archive"`, and federates a query (`search_federated([("hot", "convo"), ("warm", "docs"), ("cold", "archive")], q, 10)`). Each shard primes on miss, each shard's search runs in parallel, the global top-10 returns. The agent's result-ranker reads `result.backend` to decide trust weight (a hit in the user's docs trumps a hit in the public archive); the audit row carries the witness so the agent can prove later which bundle the hit came from. None of this requires brain-system code in ruLake — the brain owns the policy, the substrate owns the persistence.
+
+## Trust & correctness contract
+
+The contract is the agent-replayability claim: two agents on two machines querying the same data get the same byte-for-byte answer, and either one can prove what they saw to the other. The substrate acceptance test from the strategic review is the operational form: "An agent can recall, verify, forget, compact, and rehydrate memory across hot, warm, and cold tiers without knowing where the memory physically lives."
+
+The substrate acceptance test runs as a runnable test (`brain_substrate_acceptance_recall_verify_forget_rehydrate` in `tests/federation_smoke.rs`, per the resolved open question in ADR-156 §"Open questions"). It drives the six-guarantee loop — recall → verify → forget → rehydrate → location-transparency, with compact explicitly deferred per the ADR — against a single `LocalBackend`. If this test stays green on every commit, the agent-facing memory substrate claim is mechanical, not aspirational.
+
+The five primitives that satisfy the test, with citations.
+
+| Guarantee | Substrate primitive | Citation |
+|-----------|---------------------|----------|
+| Recall | `RuLake::search_one`, `search_federated` | `src/lake.rs:475`, `:521` |
+| Verify | `RuLakeBundle::verify_witness` | `src/bundle.rs:191` |
+| Forget | `invalidate_cache` + `Consistency::Fresh` re-prime | `src/lake.rs:164`; `Consistency` at `src/cache.rs` |
+| Rehydrate | `prime` on cache miss (transparent); `warm_from_dir` for explicit rehydration | `src/lake.rs:408` |
+| Location-transparency | `SearchResult` carries `backend` + `collection`; caller never touches `data_ref` | `src/lake.rs:26`-`32` |
+
+The replayability claim sits on the witness recipe (ADR-155's load-bearing property): `compute_witness` (`src/bundle.rs:362`) is SHAKE-256(32) over a domain-prefixed, length-prefixed, variant-tagged concatenation of `(data_ref, dim, rotation_seed, rerank_factor, generation)`. Two agents that agree on those five inputs derive the same witness independently and share the cache through `src/cache.rs::install_prebuilt_interned`. The agent-replayability claim therefore reduces to "do the two agents agree on the five inputs?", which is a property of the bundle, not of ruLake — and the bundle is a 300-byte sidecar that can be transported by IPFS (`ipfs-backend/`), by GCS (`gcs-backend/`), or by a tarball over scp, with the same trust property.
+
+The `memory_class` tag at `src/bundle.rs:143`-`154` is deliberately *not* part of the witness. The doc comment is explicit: "Not part of the witness: changing the memory-class tag on the same vectors does not invalidate a cache entry. Two bundles with identical data but different classes share the cache." This is the load-bearing design: the brain layer can re-classify memories without invalidating the substrate, and the substrate can serve mixed-class federations without per-class cache shards.
+
+## Reference implementation status
+
+The ADR is positioning. The reference implementation status is mostly "what was already shipped under ADR-155 M1 and what nomenclature was added on top."
+
+What shipped from ADR-155 M1 (already in scope before ADR-156 opened): `BackendAdapter` trait, `LocalBackend`, `FsBackend`, `VectorCache` with witness-addressed sharing, `Consistency::Fresh` and `Consistency::Eventual { ttl_ms }`, `RuLake::search_one` and `search_federated`, `RuLakeBundle::new`/`verify_witness`/`from_json`, `RuLake::publish_bundle`/`refresh_from_bundle_dir`, `RuLake::save_cache_to_dir`/`warm_from_dir`, `CacheStats::hit_rate`/`avg_prime_ms`. All citable in `src/lake.rs:1`-`713`, `src/cache.rs`, `src/bundle.rs:1`-`615`, `src/backend.rs`.
+
+What ADR-156 added on top, the small bits.
+
+- **`Consistency::Frozen`** shipped 2026-04-22, the "Frozen for audit" mode. Caller asserts the bundle's witness is immutable for the cache's lifetime; explicit `refresh_from_bundle_dir` still works, only the automatic coherence check is suppressed.
+- **`memory_class: Option<String>`** on the bundle, shipped at `src/bundle.rs:143`-`154`. Caller-defined, opaque, deserialises unchanged from old bundles via `serde(default, skip_serializing_if = "Option::is_none")`. Not part of the witness by design.
+- **The substrate acceptance test** as a runnable test at `tests/federation_smoke.rs::brain_substrate_acceptance_recall_verify_forget_rehydrate`, per the resolved open question. Drives recall → verify → forget → rehydrate → location-transparency against a `LocalBackend`.
+- **The framing in user-facing surfaces.** The README's "Why agents in particular" section (lines 19-24) describes ruLake to agent developers in substrate terms — single MCP tool, witness-anchored same-answer-everywhere claim, refusal as a feature. The App Store screen at `ui/src/components/screens.jsx:2018`-`2141` lists substrates as the user-facing apex of the framing — each substrate a `BackendAdapter` impl, each substrate's results carrying the same SHAKE-256 witness as a native ruLake bundle. The deep gists at `docs/gists/` (this one and the rvDNA / ruQu / IPFS / Console / datalake-layer companions) are the canonical extended record.
+
+What is *not* in scope for ADR-156 and stays in ADR-155 / ADR-007 / ADR-008 / RVM / Cognitum. Compaction (RVM/Cognitum). Cryptographic forget (ADR-155 GDPR follow-up, v2). Memory classification semantics (brain-layer concern). Recall policy beyond vector similarity (brain-layer). Trust graphs, contradiction scoring, mincut boundaries (brain-layer). Per-memory-class stats (`CacheStats::by_class`) — flagged as scope-creep toward brain features; deferred until a consumer asks.
+
+## Composition with the rest of ruLake
+
+ADR-156 is a composition statement, not a code commit, so the composition story is mostly "what already composes."
+
+The substrate framing composes with the data-lake layer (ADR-155) directly: every `BackendAdapter` impl is a substrate, every bundle's witness is the cache key, every federation is a fan-out across substrates. The substrate framing composes with the IPFS distribution layer (ADR-005): a bundle pinned to a CID is a substrate handoff between agent processes — one agent publishes, the other agent's `IpfsBackend::current_bundle` fetches, verifies, and primes. The substrate framing composes with the MCP server (ADR-004): an agent calls `rulake_query` and gets a `SearchResult` with a `backend` and a `collection` and a witness-verifiable `rulake://bundle/...` resource.
+
+The substrate framing composes with rvDNA v2 (ADR-007) and ruQu v2 (ADR-008) by giving them a memory-class label. rvDNA bundles carry `memory_class = "genomic"`; ruQu bundles carry `memory_class = "quantum"` (or `"quantum-simulation"` per the v2 spec). Each is a substrate impl that ruLake hosts without modification — the trait, the bundle, the witness, the federation primitive, the audit pipeline are all unchanged. The substrate framing is the contract that lets these external substrates land without a federation rewrite.
+
+The substrate framing composes with the Console (ADR-006) at the App Store screen. Each substrate gets a card (`ui/src/components/screens.jsx:2018`-`2141`) listing install commands, the ADR that governs it, status, the MCP tools it adds. The HelpStrip text on the App Store screen is the user-facing canonical record of the substrate framing: "Substrates are crates that plug into ruLake's witness-anchored cache via the `BackendAdapter` trait and optionally expose an MCP tool surface alongside `rulake-mcp`. Every substrate's results carry the same SHAKE-256 witness as a native ruLake bundle — federation and audit work without per-substrate special-casing."
+
+A consumer, in this framing, is an agent system that wires the substrate without owning it. The consumer owns memory-class semantics, recall policy, mutation policy. The substrate owns recall, verify, forget, rehydrate, location-transparency, and the witness. The two compose because the boundary is the trait and the bundle, not a framework.
+
+## Open questions
+
+Where does `memory_class` live — on the bundle, on the cache entry, or both? Currently bundle (travels with the witness); a cache-entry override might be useful for mixed-class federation. Defer until a consumer asks. Do we expose per-memory-class stats? `CacheStats::by_class` as a `HashMap<String, CacheStats>` would let a brain operator see hit rate per memory type — tiny change, but scope-creep toward brain features. Is `Frozen` the right default for bundles produced by RVF ingest jobs? Probably yes — the ingest job already witness-signs the output — but codify the handoff rather than leaving it to convention. The substrate acceptance test was an open question and got resolved as a runnable test (`brain_substrate_acceptance_recall_verify_forget_rehydrate`); the remaining honest unknown is how the test extends when a real brain-system consumer materialises (does compact need a partial form? does forget need a per-memory-class shape?). Each is honest about being unresolved, and none block the substrate framing from being adopted.
